3A_Bibliographic.citation: Paulich, K. N., Ross, J. M., Lessem, J. M., & Hewitt, J. K. (2021). Screen time and early adolescent mental health, academic, and social outcomes in 9- and 10-year old children: Utilizing the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. PLoS ONE, 16(9), e0256591.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly named; baseline data collection completed October 2018 (NIMH Data Archive DOI 10.15154/1519271)
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Participants were drawn from the 11,875 children enrolled in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. We utilized the entire existing dataset because the large sample size, which was determined by the design of the ABCD Study, ensures sufficient power to detect even small associations.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: N=11,727 for Part 1 (weekday) and N=11,672 for Part 2 (weekend) after exclusions for implausible values.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Total recreational screen time (hours/day) on weekdays (Part 1) and weekends (Part 2), plus individual modalities
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) - youth self-report (14 items); Parent STQ for parent-reported total
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat, mature games, R-rated movies
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (0-4+ per modality; total is composite across 6 media types)
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for modality-specific; parent-report for total
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: A 14-question Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) was completed by the children, providing self-report measures of screen time use, divided by weekdays and weekends. The questionnaire asks how many hours per weekday/weekend day the child uses screens for different types of media, with responses ranging from '0 h' (0) to '4 + h' (4). The STQ divides screen time use among six different forms of recreational (not for schoolwork) media use: television shows and movies, videos, video games, texting, social media, and video chat.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Multiple outcomes: depression, anxiety, internalizing, externalizing, ODD, conduct disorder, attention problems, ADHD, academic performance, sleep quantity, sleep quality, peer relationships
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) T-scores for mental health/behavioral outcomes; parent-reported grades for academic performance; parent-reported sleep quantity and Sleep Disturbance Scale total for sleep quality; youth-reported number of close friends
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Participants' depression symptoms were evaluated on a subscale containing 13 statements... The resulting CBCL derived T-score for depression was used for analysis; subsequent CBCL measures of interest were also analyzed via T-scores.
3D_Outcome.notes: 13 different outcome measures examined across multiple domains.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple linear regression controlling for SES and race/ethnicity, run separately by sex; interaction tests for sex; Pearson correlations; separate analyses for weekday (Part 1) and weekend (Part 2)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple linear regressions were then run, separately by sex, with the different measures for screen time as the predictor and each outcome variable as the dependent variable. All regressions controlled for both SES and race/ethnicity... Thirteen primary regressions were conducted; to account for multiple testing, the Bonferroni corrected significance level was .004 for our primary test.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: ADHD Males: Standardized Beta 0.102, t=7.34, p<.001, Partial Correlation .098
ADHD Females: Standardized Beta 0.097, t=6.80, p<.001, Partial Correlation .094

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Ordinal sleep quantity (recoded: higher = more sleep); partial correlation = -.171

Sleep quantity Females: Standardized Beta -0.143, t=-10.56, p<.001, Partial Correlation -.146

Academic performance Females: Standardized Beta -0.118, t=-8.27, p<.001, Partial Correlation -.119
Externalizing Males: Standardized Beta 0.076, t=5.48, p<.001, Partial Correlation .073
Externalizing Females: Standardized Beta 0.091, t=6.35, p<.001, Partial Correlation .088

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CBCL T-score; not significant at Bonferroni-corrected .004
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Depression Males: Standardized Beta 0.036, t=2.58, p=0.010, Partial Correlation .035

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CBCL T-score; not significant at Bonferroni-corrected .004
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Depression Females: Standardized Beta 0.029, t=2.03, p=0.042, Partial Correlation .028

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: CBCL T-score; not significant at Bonferroni-corrected .004

Anxiety Females: Standardized Beta 0.000, t=0.002, p=0.999, Partial Correlation .000

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Number of close female friends - Males
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Count of close friends; partial correlation = .076
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Num. of close f. friends Males: Standardized Beta 0.079, t=5.69, p<.001, Partial Correlation .076
ADHD Males weekend: Standardized Beta 0.126, t=9.25, p<.001, Partial Correlation .123
ADHD Females weekend: Standardized Beta 0.131, t=9.32, p<.001, Partial Correlation .129

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: We utilized the entire existing dataset because the large sample size, which was determined by the design of the ABCD Study, ensures sufficient power to detect even small associations.
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: effect sizes associated with screen time and the various outcomes were modest; SES was more strongly associated with each outcome measure. Our analyses do not establish causality and the small effect sizes observed suggest that increased screen time is unlikely to be directly harmful to 9-and-10-year-old children.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper explicitly acknowledges small effects (<2% variance explained), compares unfavorably to SES effects (~5%), and concludes screen time is unlikely to be directly harmful. This is an unusually appropriate acknowledgment of effect size magnitude. Bonferroni correction applied.

3H_Recommendations.notes: Paper explicitly avoids clinical/public health recommendations, instead concluding that screen time effects are small and unlikely to be directly harmful.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion is notably measured and cautious. It emphasizes that screen time effects are small (<2% variance), much smaller than SES effects (~5%). It explicitly states screen time is unlikely to be directly harmful at this age. The paper discusses reverse causality possibilities (e.g., parents of children with externalizing problems may use screens as distraction). It frames null findings for depression and anxiety as important and suggests that screen time effects on internalizing problems may emerge at older ages. The conclusion is remarkably restrained for a screen time study.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: CIs not reported for regression coefficients. Only standardized betas, t-statistics, p-values, and partial correlations reported. Many effect sizes extracted from supplementary tables are not included here - only primary total screen time effects extracted.
